summary: Deprecated audit trail for the Windows Terminal internal-flag bundle. The current app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows Terminal\DeveloperMode and EnableDebugTap, but this research pass did not capture a published registry contract for either value.

setting.name: Windows Terminal Developer Features
setting.casual_explanation: The app tries to turn on extra Windows Terminal developer-style features. This research pass did not capture those features as documented registry settings in Windows Terminal's public settings surface.
setting.why_people_change_it: People would only change it when experimenting with Windows Terminal internals or trying to expose extra development behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: This is not a normal end-user terminal setting, and this pass did not capture a primary Windows Terminal registry contract for the values written by the app.
setting.professional_notes: Windows Terminal's public documentation focuses on settings.json and documented settings categories. The current app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows Terminal\DeveloperMode = 1 and EnableDebugTap = 1, but this pass did not capture a primary Windows Terminal source publishing those registry values as supported controls.

allowed_values.label: Primary Terminal mapping not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a Windows Terminal source that publishes DeveloperMode as a supported registry setting.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app writes 1 here to enable an internal developer-mode style flag for Windows Terminal.

allowed_values.label: Primary Terminal mapping not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a Windows Terminal source that publishes EnableDebugTap as a supported registry setting.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app writes 1 here to enable an internal debug-tap style flag for Windows Terminal.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DeveloperMode = 1 and EnableDebugTap = 1, but this pass did not capture a primary Windows Terminal registry contract for those values.

windows_defaults.label: Conservative baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows Terminal profiles where this dataset has not validated the registry contract for internal developer flags

states.rationale: Do not publish DeveloperMode as validated until a primary Windows Terminal source is captured.
Do not publish EnableDebugTap as validated until a primary Windows Terminal source is captured.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps internal Terminal flags out of published presets until they are properly sourced.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

states.rationale: Keep undocumented registry flags blocked until a primary source is captured.
Keep undocumented registry flags blocked until a primary source is captured.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the current experimental behavior without publishing it as a validated Windows Terminal setting.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a fully sourced Windows Terminal configuration record.
tradeoffs: The registry flags remain blocked from validated publication.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Observed registry flag written by the app.
Observed registry flag written by the app.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting the app's current Terminal behavior for later cleanup.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a sourced Windows Terminal settings implementation.
tradeoffs: The current registry flags are not validated as the public Windows Terminal control surface.
recommended_profiles.notes: Blocked until a primary Windows Terminal source for the registry values is captured or the app moves to a documented settings surface.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Terminal settings
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents Windows Terminal through settings.json and documented settings pages. This research pass did not capture a primary registry contract for DeveloperMode or EnableDebugTap.

evidence.title: Ghidra headless raw-memory scan of TerminalApp.dll
evidence.summary: Headless Ghidra raw-memory scans of TerminalApp.dll wrote NO_MATCH for DeveloperMode and EnableDebugTap, so the flags are not present as plain strings in the imported binary.

evidence.title: WPR capture of Windows Terminal launch
evidence.summary: GeneralProfile WPR capture of wt.exe launch captured the startup sequence for manual trace review. This is supporting runtime context, not registry-semantic proof.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DeveloperMode = 1 and EnableDebugTap = 1 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows Terminal.

decision.why: VM corroboration was backfilled with Ghidra raw-memory scans and a WPR launch capture, but this research pass still did not capture a primary Windows Terminal source for the exact registry values written by the app.
blocking_issues: No primary Windows Terminal source for DeveloperMode or EnableDebugTap was captured in this pass.
The public Windows Terminal documentation captured here focuses on settings.json rather than the registry flags written by the app.
The Ghidra scan only provides negative string evidence, not a documented registry contract.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Microsoft Learn: Windows Terminal settings: Microsoft documents Windows Terminal through settings.json and documented settings pages. This research pass did not capture a primary registry contract for DeveloperMode or EnableDebugTap.
validation_proof.notes: Backfilled from evidence_id windows-terminal-settings-doc (official-doc); deprecated audit trail.
